context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Corruption and Reform of Public Institutions

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Is corruption-free development ever possible? How does one understand claims of Asian distinctiveness about corruption – the 'grease the wheel' thesis – against the Asian successes in rooting out corruption as in Hong Kong? What does corruption signify about the State, its legitimacy and the ensemble of techniques through which development interventions occur?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on a 700–800 word reflection for my development planning class about corruption and reform of public institutions. I’m a little unsure how to answer the prompt without making it too broad, so I’d like help narrowing the argument.
exchanges.tutor: Good place to start! Before we think about narrowing, what's your initial gut reaction to the central question: do you think corruption-free development is ever possible? Start there — your instinct will help us find your angle.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting and asking for help narrowing their argument. Rather than giving them a direction, I should use a Socratic question to get them thinking about what they already know and what angle interests them most. This keeps the discovery student-led from the start.

exchanges.student: My first instinct is probably no, not completely corruption-free, because that feels kind of unrealistic in any state. But I’m not sure if that sounds too cynical, and I don’t know how to connect that to development planning yet.
exchanges.tutor: That instinct is worth pursuing — cynical doesn't mean wrong. Now think about this: if corruption is never fully eliminated, what does that tell us about the *state* itself? What is the state's relationship to corruption, in your view?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a genuine instinct but is second-guessing themselves. I should validate that their instinct is worth exploring without endorsing it as correct, and push them to connect it to the course themes rather than doing that work for them. A Socratic question pointing toward the state/legitimacy angle in the prompt is the right move here.

exchanges.student: I think it might mean the state isn’t just a neutral actor enforcing rules, because people inside it use power in uneven ways. Maybe corruption shows weakness in institutions, but I’m confused because sometimes strong states are corrupt too, so I’m not sure how to phrase that well.
exchanges.tutor: That tension you've noticed is actually really important — hold onto it. So if both weak *and* strong states can be corrupt, what might be the deciding factor? Think about the Hong Kong example the prompt mentions: what was different there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a genuinely good observation about the state not being neutral and is noticing a contradiction (strong states can also be corrupt). This is productive tension. I should affirm the tension is real and worth exploring, and push them to think about what distinguishes corrupt strong states from clean strong states, which will naturally lead them toward the legitimacy angle in the prompt and potentially the Hong Kong case.

exchanges.student: I guess the difference wasn’t just “state strength” by itself, but how power was organized and constrained. For Hong Kong, I think the state had enough capacity to enforce rules, but also some legitimacy and institutions that made anti-corruption efforts credible. Can you help me say that more clearly without making it sound too vague?
exchanges.tutor: I can't help you phrase it — that part is yours to work out! But ask yourself this: what specifically made anti-corruption efforts *credible* in Hong Kong? What did people have to believe about the state for those efforts to work?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to help them write or polish their argument, which I should refuse per the rules. However, the underlying idea is good and developing well. I should redirect them back to doing the work themselves while pushing them to think more precisely about what 'legitimacy' and 'credible institutions' actually mean in the Hong Kong context — this will naturally sharpen their own language.

exchanges.student: I think people had to believe the rules would apply to powerful people too, not just ordinary citizens. Maybe credibility came from enforcement being more consistent and from the state seeming less captured by private interests. I’m still kinda guessing though.
exchanges.tutor: That's not guessing — that's a coherent argument about legitimacy. Now, the prompt also asks about the 'grease the wheel' thesis and Asian distinctiveness. How does that idea sit with what you just said about equal enforcement?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is developing a solid intuition about equal enforcement and state capture, which connects well to legitimacy. They're underselling themselves by saying they're 'guessing.' I should affirm the quality of their thinking and now push them to connect this to the 'grease the wheel' thesis mentioned in the prompt, which they haven't addressed yet. This moves the conversation forward toward covering all parts of the prompt.

exchanges.student: I think the “grease the wheel” idea kind of treats corruption like a practical shortcut when formal institutions are slow or rigid. But that seems to clash with equal enforcement, because if bribery helps things move, it usually helps people with money or connections more. I’m not fully sure if I should reject the thesis completely or say it explains something limited.
exchanges.tutor: Good — you've spotted a real tension. So think about it this way: what would be the *consequences* of each position for your overall argument? Does one fit better with the evidence you've already built up about Hong Kong and legitimacy?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is doing well and has identified a real tension in the 'grease the wheel' thesis. They're asking whether to fully reject or partially accept it — this is a genuine analytical choice they need to make for their argument. I should push them to think through the implications of each position rather than suggesting which one to take. This keeps the discovery student-led.

exchanges.student: I think saying it explains something limited fits better. If I fully accept “grease the wheel,” then Hong Kong’s anti-corruption success matters less, but if I fully reject it, I might ignore why corruption can persist in fast-growing systems. I’d rather argue that corruption may sometimes speed transactions in the short term, but it weakens legitimacy and fair development over time.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong central argument — it's nuanced and it handles the prompt's tensions well. You now have several key ideas: your skepticism about corruption-free development, the state/legitimacy point, the Hong Kong case, and your qualified take on 'grease the wheel.' How are you thinking about organizing these into a coherent 700–800 word piece?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a nuanced and well-reasoned position that holds both parts of the tension together. This is a strong thesis-level insight. We're about 6 exchanges in and they now have the core building blocks of their argument. I should affirm this progress and push them to now think about how to structure all of this into their 700-800 word reflection, moving toward integration.

exchanges.student: I’m thinking intro with my main claim, then one paragraph on why fully corruption-free development is probably unrealistic, one on the “grease the wheel” idea, one on Hong Kong as a counterexample, and a last one on what corruption shows about the state and legitimacy. I’m not sure if that order is the best though, especially because the legitimacy point feels like it should maybe come earlier.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct to question the order. Ask yourself: is the legitimacy point a *conclusion* you're building toward, or is it more of a *lens* that shapes how you interpret everything else, including Hong Kong and 'grease the wheel'?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid outline and is self-critically noticing that the legitimacy point might need repositioning. Rather than telling them where to put it, I should ask them to think about what role legitimacy plays in their overall argument — is it a conclusion or a lens? This will help them decide the structure themselves.

exchanges.student: I think it’s more of a lens, not just a conclusion. So maybe I should bring legitimacy in earlier, because it helps explain both why corruption persists and why anti-corruption reforms sometimes work. Could you help me test a better order for the paragraphs?
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly the right insight — if legitimacy is your lens, it should probably frame the whole piece. Now try it yourself: given that, which paragraph would you move, and where?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to do the organizational work for them. I should redirect while affirming their insight about legitimacy as a lens. Since they've correctly identified legitimacy as a framing concept, I should push them to try reordering the paragraphs themselves based on that insight.

exchanges.student: I’d move the state/legitimacy paragraph right after the intro, so the order would be: intro and claim, then legitimacy as the lens, then why corruption-free development is unlikely, then the limited “grease the wheel” point, then Hong Kong as evidence that corruption can be reduced when institutions are credible. I think that makes more sense, but I’m still a little worried Hong Kong should come before the grease thesis.
exchanges.tutor: Your outline is solid either way — this is a minor ordering choice that won't make or break the piece. You have your claim, your lens, and your key examples. The best way to test the order now is to actually start writing. What's your opening sentence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done a good job reorganizing and is now second-guessing the Hong Kong vs. grease-the-wheel order. This is a minor structural detail and they've been going back and forth on structure for a few exchanges. It's time to push them to stop deliberating and start writing, since they have all the ingredients. I should affirm their outline and encourage them to move to drafting.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic method throughout all ten exchanges. No direct answers were given; instead, the tutor consistently posed targeted questions that guided the student to generate their own ideas about legitimacy, the 'grease the wheel' thesis, and the Hong Kong case. Scaffolding was well-paced, building on each student response before advancing. Responses were concise and appropriately bite-sized. Tone was supportive and formative throughout.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions are warranted across any criterion. The tutor never provided submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never stated conclusions before the student reasoned toward them. Each turn built directly on the student's prior response, demonstrating strong scaffolding. The tutor gave process-level feedback (e.g., distinguishing 'lens' from 'conclusion' in Turn 8-9) rather than merely validating correctness. Dialogue progressed without repetition or off-topic drift, and all responses were appropriately concise. Tone was consistently encouraging without being evaluative or grade-implying.
